Automating Git workflows with advanced bash scripts
Production-grade bash scripts for multi-branch Git strategies: automated rebasing, conflict detection, branch hygiene, and CI integration.

Git is the backbone of every software delivery pipeline. Yet most teams use it interactively, manually, repetitively. Automating the repetitive parts — branch sync, rebase chains, cleanup — saves hours per week and eliminates entire classes of human error.

## Branch Hygiene Script

Delete merged branches locally and remotely:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

MAIN_BRANCH="${1:-main}"
REMOTE="${2:-origin}"

echo "→ Fetching and pruning..."
git fetch "$REMOTE" --prune

echo "→ Deleting merged local branches..."
git branch --merged "$MAIN_BRANCH" \
  | grep -vE "^\*|$MAIN_BRANCH|develop|staging" \
  | xargs -r git branch -d

echo "→ Deleting merged remote branches..."
git branch -r --merged "$REMOTE/$MAIN_BRANCH" \
  | grep -v "$MAIN_BRANCH\|HEAD\|develop\|staging" \
  | sed "s|$REMOTE/||" \
  | xargs -r -I{} git push "$REMOTE" --delete {}

echo "✓ Branch cleanup complete."
```

## Automated Rebase Chain

When you have `feature → develop → staging → main`, keeping all branches up to date manually is error-prone:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

BRANCHES=(main staging develop feature/current)

for i in "${!BRANCHES[@]}"; do
  if [[ $i -eq 0 ]]; then continue; fi
  
  PARENT="${BRANCHES[$((i-1))]}"
  CHILD="${BRANCHES[$i]}"
  
  echo "→ Rebasing $CHILD onto $PARENT..."
  git checkout "$CHILD"
  
  if ! git rebase "$PARENT"; then
    echo "✗ Conflict on $CHILD. Resolve and re-run."
    git rebase --abort
    exit 1
  fi
  
  git push --force-with-lease origin "$CHILD"
  echo "✓ $CHILD rebased and pushed."
done
```

`--force-with-lease` is the safe force push — it refuses to overwrite if someone else has pushed since you last fetched.

## Pre-Commit Validation Hook

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# .git/hooks/pre-commit

set -euo pipefail

STAGED=$(git diff --cached --name-only --diff-filter=ACM)

echo "→ Running pre-commit checks..."

# Lint
if echo "$STAGED" | grep -qE '\.(ts|tsx|js|jsx)$'; then
  npx eslint $(echo "$STAGED" | grep -E '\.(ts|tsx|js|jsx)$') || exit 1
fi

# Type check
if echo "$STAGED" | grep -qE '\.(ts|tsx)$'; then
  npx tsc --noEmit || exit 1
fi

# Detect debug statements
if git diff --cached | grep -E '^\+.*(console\.log|debugger|TODO:|FIXME:)' | grep -qv '^+++'; then
  echo "✗ Debug statements or TODO markers found in staged changes."
  exit 1
fi

echo "✓ Pre-commit checks passed."
```

## Conventional Commit Linter

Pair with `commitlint` for enforced message format:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# .git/hooks/commit-msg

COMMIT_MSG=$(cat "$1")
PATTERN="^(feat|fix|docs|style|refactor|test|chore|ci|perf|build|revert)(\(.+\))?: .{1,72}$"

if ! echo "$COMMIT_MSG" | grep -qE "$PATTERN"; then
  echo "✗ Commit message format invalid."
  echo "  Expected: type(scope): description"
  echo "  Example:  feat(auth): add JWT refresh token rotation"
  exit 1
fi
```

## CI Integration

Use GitHub Actions to validate branch naming:

```yaml
- name: Validate branch name
  run: |
    BRANCH="${{ github.head_ref }}"
    PATTERN="^(feat|fix|docs|chore|refactor|test|ci)/[a-z0-9-]+$"
    if ! echo "$BRANCH" | grep -qE "$PATTERN"; then
      echo "Branch name '$BRANCH' does not follow convention."
      exit 1
    fi
```

Automation turns Git from a version control tool into a delivery pipeline primitive.

Rust microservices on AWS EKS: minimal viable config
Deploying Rust services to Amazon EKS with the minimum footprint: multi-stage Docker builds, IAM roles, S3 access, and Secrets Manager integration.

Rust microservices on EKS have a compelling story: small binaries, near-zero memory footprint, no GC pauses. But getting the AWS integration right takes some ceremony. Here's the minimal config that actually works in production.

## Multi-Stage Docker Build

```dockerfile
FROM rust:1.76-slim as builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY Cargo.toml Cargo.lock ./
COPY src ./src
RUN cargo build --release

FROM gcr.io/distroless/cc-debian12
COPY --from=builder /app/target/release/my-service /usr/local/bin/my-service
EXPOSE 8080
CMD ["/usr/local/bin/my-service"]
```

The distroless base gives you a ~10MB final image with no shell attack surface.

## EKS Pod Identity for S3 Access

Use IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts) — never mount static credentials:

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: my-service
  annotations:
    eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::123456789:role/my-service-role
```

IAM policy:
```json
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [{
    "Effect": "Allow",
    "Action": ["s3:GetObject", "s3:PutObject"],
    "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/*"
  }]
}
```

In Rust, the `aws-sdk-rust` automatically picks up the IRSA token via the default credentials chain:

```rust
use aws_config::BehaviorVersion;
use aws_sdk_s3::Client;

let config = aws_config::load_defaults(BehaviorVersion::latest()).await;
let s3 = Client::new(&config);
```

## Secrets Manager Integration

```rust
use aws_sdk_secretsmanager::Client as SecretsClient;

async fn get_secret(client: &SecretsClient, name: &str) -> anyhow::Result<String> {
    let resp = client
        .get_secret_value()
        .secret_id(name)
        .send()
        .await?;
    Ok(resp.secret_string().unwrap_or_default().to_string())
}
```

Cache secrets at startup. Reload on SIGHUP to avoid pod restarts for rotation.

## Kubernetes Deployment

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: my-service
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-service
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: my-service
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: my-service
      containers:
      - name: my-service
        image: 123456789.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/my-service:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "32Mi"
            cpu: "50m"
          limits:
            memory: "64Mi"
            cpu: "200m"
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8080
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
```

32MB memory request for a production Rust service. That number alone justifies the learning curve.

## Minimal Rust Health Endpoint

```rust
use axum::{routing::get, Router};

#[tokio::main]
async fn main() {
    let app = Router::new().route("/health", get(|| async { "ok" }));
    let listener = tokio::net::TcpListener::bind("0.0.0.0:8080").await.unwrap();
    axum::serve(listener, app).await.unwrap();
}
```

This is the baseline. From here you add domain logic, tracing (`tracing` + `opentelemetry`), and metrics (`metrics` crate).
A suite of bash scripts and GitHub Actions workflows for enforcing Git conventions, automating branch management, and integrating commit validation into CI pipelines.

## Overview

A collection of production-hardened scripts and workflows that enforce consistent Git practices across a multi-team engineering organization. Adopted by 8 teams, reducing merge conflicts and CI failures caused by branch management issues by 70%.

## Components

### Branch Hygiene Automation
Scheduled GitHub Action that runs nightly to:
- Delete merged feature branches (local and remote)
- Report stale branches (no commits in 30 days)
- Post summary to Slack channel

### Conventional Commit Enforcement
- `commit-msg` hook: validates commit message format client-side
- GitHub Actions check: validates all commits in a PR
- Auto-generated changelog on release using `conventional-changelog`

### Rebase Chain Manager
Script for teams using a `feature → develop → staging → main` flow:
- Detects when parent branch has moved ahead
- Automatically rebases child branches in order
- Uses `--force-with-lease` to avoid overwriting concurrent pushes
- Aborts and notifies on conflict

### Branch Naming Enforcer
GitHub Actions workflow that:
- Validates PR branch names against pattern `type/description`
- Comments on the PR with correction instructions if invalid
- Required check before merge is allowed

## Impact

- Merge conflict rate: -70% (branches stay current)
- CI failures from branch issues: -85%
- Onboarding time for Git conventions: 1 day → 2 hours (scripts do the work)
End-to-end point-of-sale and payment processing platform for restaurant chains, including CFDI 4.0 tax compliance, Kafka-based event streaming, and multi-tenant architecture.

## Overview

A distributed POS platform serving 50+ restaurant locations, processing thousands of transactions daily with full CFDI 4.0 (Mexican electronic invoice) compliance built into the payment flow.

## Architecture

The system is structured around bounded contexts:

- **Order Context**: takes orders, manages item lifecycle, publishes `OrderPlaced` / `OrderUpdated` events
- **Payment Context**: subscribes to order events, orchestrates payment providers, issues CFDI documents
- **Inventory Context**: updates stock levels reactively from order events
- **Reporting Context**: projects events into analytics-ready read models

All inter-context communication flows through Kafka. No direct service-to-service HTTP calls except for synchronous user-facing APIs.

## Key Technical Decisions

**Kafka for reliability**: The event log serves as the source of truth for order state. If the payment service restarts mid-transaction, it replays from its last committed offset.

**CFDI compliance as a domain service**: The tax document generation logic is encapsulated behind a `CfdiService` port, with adapters for both the SAT (Mexican Tax Authority) web service and a local XML-signing library.

**Multi-tenancy at the data layer**: Each restaurant chain gets a dedicated PostgreSQL schema. The application resolves the schema from the JWT tenant claim. No cross-tenant queries possible at the ORM level.

## Outcomes

- Zero data loss across 18 months in production
- Payment reconciliation time reduced from 4 hours/day (manual) to 15 minutes (automated)
- CFDI compliance rate: 99.97% (failures are retried with dead-letter queue)
Production deployment of Rust-based microservices on Amazon EKS using Distroless containers, IRSA for S3 and Secrets Manager access, and a full GitOps CI/CD pipeline.

## Overview

A suite of lightweight Rust microservices running on Amazon EKS, each with sub-100MB memory footprint. The project demonstrates that Rust is viable for production cloud services beyond systems programming.

## Services

- **Document Processor**: reads from S3, transforms documents, writes results back. Handles 10,000 documents/hour on a single 2-vCPU pod.
- **Notification Dispatcher**: subscribes to SNS topics, dispatches emails and SMS via third-party APIs with retry and circuit breaker logic.
- **Auth Gateway**: validates JWT tokens, forwards requests with enriched headers. ~2ms p99 latency.

## Infrastructure Design

Each service follows the same pattern:
1. Multi-stage Docker build → distroless image (~12MB)
2. Kubernetes ServiceAccount annotated with IRSA role ARN
3. AWS SDK credential chain picks up IAM role automatically
4. Secrets loaded from Secrets Manager at startup, cached in-process
5. Health endpoints at `/health/live` and `/health/ready`

## CI/CD Pipeline

```
Push → GitHub Actions → cargo test → cargo clippy → docker build → 
ECR push → kubectl rollout (staging) → smoke test → kubectl rollout (prod)
```

Rollback is a single `kubectl rollout undo` command, automated when smoke tests fail.

## Results

- Image size: 12–18MB per service (vs 250–400MB for equivalent JVM services)
- Cold start: <500ms (vs 5–15s for JVM)
- Memory usage: 20–45MB per pod at load
- Cost reduction: ~60% vs equivalent JVM workload on same node class
